a minimum-cost path from one graph vertex to another. (all of these do)
a parenthesization of a matrix product that maximizes the total multiplication
a maximum-cost path from one graph vertex to another.
correctAnswer: a maximum-cost path from one graph vertex to another.

Which of the following is not a desirable property of a hash function h(x)?
options: a)   It should be computable in O(1) time.
b) The range of h(x) should stay within the desired hash-table size.
c)   The range of h(x) should include a wide range of integers.
d) If x1,...., xn are the items to be hashed, then the numbers h(x1),....,h(xn)
correctAnswer: a)   It should be computable in O(1) time.

questionText: If a separate-chaining hash table has load factor = 5, then the average length of a chain equals
Perfect hashing
options: a)   may be applied to static hash tables.
b) means there are no collisions when retrieving data from the hash table.
c)   represents one of the most efficient ways of retrieving data.
correctAnswer: a)   may be applied to static hash tables.

Which of the following graph problems cannot be solved in time that is linear with respect to the sum of the number of vertices and edges in the graph (i.e. O(m + n)).
options: a)   determining if a simple graph is connected
b)  determining if a simple graph is bipartite
c)   determining a minimum spanning tree for a connected weighted graph
d)  determining a topological sort of the vertices for a directed acyclic graph
correctAnswer: a)   determining if a simple graph is connected

questionText: Suppose directed graph G is weakly but not strongly connected, and path P = a,b, c,d provides a weak (but not strong) connection from a to d within G. Then we surely know that
options: a)   either (a, b), or (b, c), or (c, d) is an edge of G.
b)  (a,b), and (b, c), and (c, d) are edges of G.
c)   either (b,a), or (c, b), or (d, c) is an edge of G.
d)  (b,a), and (c, b), and (d, c) are edges of G.
correctAnswer: a)   either (a, b), or (b, c), or (c, d) is an edge of G.

questionText: Let G = (V, E) be the undirected graph whose edges are {(a,b), (b, c), (c, d), (a,d)}. If T is the depth- first spanning tree of G rooted at vertex a, it follows that T has ______ branch(es).
The worst case running time T (n) for inserting n elements into an initially-empty binary search tree is
Binary search tree T is said to be balanced when, for every node n in T ,
options: a)   n’s left and right subtrees have equal height.
b)  n’s left and right subtrees have equal size.
c)   n’s left and right subtrees have heights that differ by at most one.
d) n’s left and right subtrees have sizes that differ by at most one.
correctAnswer: a)   n’s left and right subtrees have equal height.

questionText: If numbers from the set {1,...., n} are selected at random (without replacement) and inserted into an initially-empty binary search tree, then the big-O expression that best describes the average (taken over all possible resulting trees) height of the resulting tree is
Which of the following sorting tasks would not be appropriate for Counting Sort? Fall 2020
options: a)   sorting millions of products by their 15-digit serial numbers
b) sorting millions of employees by their ages
c)   sorting each day of the calendar year by the high temperature (in Celsius
d) sorting each vehicle registered in the state of California by the year it was
correctAnswer: a)   sorting millions of products by their 15-digit serial numbers

questionText: Which of the following best describes the worst-case running time of Insertion Sort when sorting n 32-bit unsigned integers?
Quicksort is guaranteed to run in time O(n log n) so long as the pivot is
options: b) set to the median of the  rst, middle, and last array element.
c)   set to the median of the array.

questionText: When following Kruskal’s algorithm, the greedy choice is to
options: a)   remove the edge of greatest cost from the graph so long as its removal does
b) add the edge of least cost to the forest so long as its addition does not
c)   add the vertex having least connection cost to the current tree.
d) remove the vertex having greatest connection cost from the tree.
correctAnswer: a)   remove the edge of greatest cost from the graph so long as its removal does

questionText: When following Huffman’s algorithm, the greedy choice is to
options: a)   select the two probabilities of least value for merging.
b) select the two probabilities of greatest value for merging.
c)   add the codeword of shortest length to the code being constructed.
d) remove the codeword of greatest length from the code being constructed.
correctAnswer: a)   select the two probabilities of least value for merging.

b) storing solutions to smaller subproblems for future look up when solving
c)   the ability of a dynamic-programming algorithm to remember the current
d) the ability to remember dynamic-programming recurrences for a comprehensive

questionText: All of the following problems can be solved with a dynamic-programming algorithm that runs in worst-case polynomial time, except for fall 2020
The Floyd-Warshall dynamic-programming algorithm defines the matrix of numbers dkij, where i and j are graph vertices satisfying 1<=i; j<=n, andk ∈ {0, 1, . . . , n}. Moreover, dkij represents
options: a)   the distance from i to j when restricted to paths that traverse at most k
b) the distance from i to j when restricted to paths that traverse at least k
c)   the distance from i to j when restricted to paths whose intermediate vertices
d) the distance from i to j when restricted to paths whose intermediate vertices
correctAnswer: a)   the distance from i to j when restricted to paths that traverse at most k

questionText: The problem of determining if a bipartite graph has a perfect matching is most naturally reduced to the problem of
options: a)   finding a Hamilton cycle in a simple graph.
b) finding a vertex cover in a simple graph.
c)   finding the maximum flow in a directed network.
d) finding the longest path in a directed network.
correctAnswer: a)   finding a Hamilton cycle in a simple graph.

questionText: The problem of determining the maximum ow in a directed network is most naturally reduced to the problem of
options: a)   determining if a 0-1 Knapsack has a pro t that exceeds some threshold.
b) finding the longest path in a directed network.
c)   determining if there is a path from one vertex to another in a graph.
d) finding the longest path in an acyclic directed network.
correctAnswer: a)   determining if a 0-1 Knapsack has a pro t that exceeds some threshold.

questionText: Professor Jones has just proved that there is no polynomial-time algorithm for solving the 0-1 Knapsack optimization problem. Because of this we also know there is no polynomial-time algorithm for solving each of the following problems, except for
options: a)   determining if a Boolean formula has a satisfying assignment.
b) determining if a graph has a Hamilton cycle.
c)   determining if a set of integers has a subset that sums to some value t.
d) determining the longest path in a directed acyclic graph.
correctAnswer: a)   determining if a Boolean formula has a satisfying assignment.

questionText: The Subset Sum decision problem is most closely associated with
options: a)   the Set Partition decision problem.
c)   the Hamilton Cycle decision problem.
d) the Vertex Cover decision problem.
a) O(n). b) O(log n). c) O(√ n). d) O(n).
a) sorting millions of products by their 15-digit serial numbers
b) sorting millions of employees by their ages
c) sorting each day of the calendar year by the high temperature (in Celsius rounded to the
correctAnswer: a)   the Set Partition decision problem.

questionText: Given 2SAT instance C, which of the following statements implies that there is a unique sat- isfying assignment for C. Note: Rx denotes the reachability set for x in the implication graph GC .
options: a) For every variable x on which C depends, either Rx or Rx is consistent, but not both.
b) GC has no inconsistent cycles.
c) There is a reachability set Rx for which ↵Rx satisﬁes every clause of C.
d) None of the above imply a unique satisfying assignment.
correctAnswer: a) For every variable x on which C depends, either Rx or Rx is consistent, but not both.

questionText: Given 2SAT instance C,w h i c ho ft h ef o l l o w i n gs t a t e m e n t si se q u i v a l e n tt oC being unsatisﬁable.

b) There is no variable x for which both Rx and Rx are consistent.
c) There is a variable x for which both Rx and Rx are inconsistent.
d) None of the above are equivalent to being unsatisﬁable.
correctAnswer: a) Implication graph GC has a simple path of length 2 n�1, where n is the number of variables

options: a) determining if a simple graph is connected
b) determining if a simple graph is bipartite
c) determining a minimum spanning tree for a connected weighted graph
d) determining a topological sort of the vertices for a directed acyclic graph
correctAnswer: a) determining if a simple graph is connected

Binary search tree T is said to be balanced when, for every node n in T ,
options: a) n’s left and right subtrees have equal height.
b) n’s left and right subtrees have equal size.
c) n’s left and right subtrees have heights that di↵er by at most one.
d) n’s left and right subtrees have sizes that di↵er by at most one.
correctAnswer: a) n’s left and right subtrees have equal height.

Which of the following sorting tasks would not be appropriate for Counting Sort?
options: a) sorting millions of products by their 15-digit serial numbers
b) sorting millions of employees by their ages
c) sorting each day of the calendar year by the high temperature (in Celsius rounded to the
d) sorting each vehicle registered in the state of California by the year it was manufactured
correctAnswer: a) sorting millions of products by their 15-digit serial numbers

questionText: When following Kruskal’s algorithm, the greedy choice is to
options: a) remove the edge of greatest cost from the graph so long as its removal does not disconnect
b) add the edge of least cost to the forest so long as its addition does not create a cycle.
c) add the vertex having least connection cost to the current tree.
d) remove the vertex having greatest connection cost from the tree.
correctAnswer: a) remove the edge of greatest cost from the graph so long as its removal does not disconnect

questionText: When following Hu↵man’s algorithm, the greedy choice is to
options: a) select the two probabilities of least value for merging.
b) select the two probabilities of greatest value for merging.
c) add the codeword of shortest length to the code being constructed.
d) remove the codeword of greatest length from the code being constructed.
correctAnswer: a) select the two probabilities of least value for merging.

questionText: The running time of Strassen’s algorithm is
In dynamic-programming “memoization” refers to
options: a) storing the dynamic-programming recurrence in a static array so that it may be applied
b) storing solutions to smaller subproblems for future look up when solving larger subprob-
c) the ability of a dynamic-programming algorithm to remember the current location in the
d) the ability to remember dynamic-programming recurrences for a comprehensive exam.
correctAnswer: a) storing the dynamic-programming recurrence in a static array so that it may be applied

questionText: All of the following problems can be solved with a dynamic-programming algorithm that runs in worst-case polynomial time, except for

The Floyd-Warshall dynamic-programming algorithm deﬁnes the matrix of numbers dk ij ,w h e r e i and j are graph vertices satisfying 1  i, j  n,a n d k 2{ 0, 1,...,n }.M o r e o v e r ,dk ij represents
options: a) the distance from i to j when restricted to paths that traverse at most k edges.
b) the distance from i to j when restricted to paths that traverse at least k edges.
c) the distance from i to j when restricted to paths whose intermediate vertices must lie
d) the distance from i to j when restricted to paths whose intermediate vertices must lie
correctAnswer: a) the distance from i to j when restricted to paths that traverse at most k edges.

questionText: The problem of determining if a bipartite graph has a perfect matching is most naturally reduced to the problem of
options: a) ﬁnding a Hamilton cycle in a simple graph.
b) ﬁnding a vertex cover in a simple graph.
c) ﬁnding the maximum ﬂow in a directed network.
d) ﬁnding the longest path in a directed network.
correctAnswer: a) ﬁnding a Hamilton cycle in a simple graph.

questionText: The problem of determining the maximum ﬂow in a directed network is most naturally reduced to the problem of
options: a) determining if a 0-1 Knapsack has a proﬁt that exceeds some threshold.
b) ﬁnding the longest path in a directed network.
c) determining if there is a path from one vertex to another in a graph.
d) ﬁnding the longest path in an acyclic directed network.
correctAnswer: a) determining if a 0-1 Knapsack has a proﬁt that exceeds some threshold.

questionText: Professor Jones has just proved that there is no polynomial-time algorithm for solving the 0-1 Knapsack optimization problem. Because of this we also know there is no polynomial-time algorithm for solving each of the following problems, except for
options: a) determining if a Boolean formula has a satisfying assignment.
b) determining if a graph has a Hamilton cycle.
c) determining if a set of integers has a subset that sums to some value t.
d) determining the longest path in a directed acyclic graph.
correctAnswer: a) determining if a Boolean formula has a satisfying assignment.

questionText: The Subset Sum decision problem is most closely associated with
options: a) the Set Partition decision problem.
c) the Hamilton Cycle decision problem.
d) the Vertex Cover decision problem.
correctAnswer: a) the Set Partition decision problem.
